images to a very very inaudi incinerating much up to my aim was to explain current orthodox scientific understanding of color and color spaces individual scientists like philosophers have many different opinions about color but my main aim was to explain to illustrate the consensus of opinions embodied in the terminology of the International Lighting vocabulary published by the Commission internationale de l'Est Claridge or CIE for short this terminology has been the subject of immensely more extensive scrutiny than the normal scientific peer review process and has been repeatedly honed by successive generations of scientists over many decades the CIE was founded in 1913 and is the organization responsible for the International coordination of lighting related technical standards in relation to color the CIE established the framework of modern colorimetry or color measurement in the first decades of the 20th century various color spaces that form the basis of color management and color technology including cielab familiar as the lab space in photoshop a series of standardised light source specifications such as CIE alumina D 50 D 65 and so on various formulae used in industry for quantifying color differences and ongoing research into mathematical models that predict the color appearance of a given stimulus under different conditions the CIE International lighting vocabulary or ilv first published in 1938 and revised periodically since then gives definitions for more than 1,400 terms and is by far the most comprehensive and authoritative source on the scientific terminology of light and color although previously very expensive and limited in distribution the ilv is now freely available online unfortunately the purely verbal definitions and brief accompanying notes and formulae in the ilv can be difficult to understand for non specialists and the explanations and illustrations in my chapter and in in this video are meant to help remedy this in this video I'll be looking at the fundamental questions of what is a color and in what sense can the colors of lights and objects be measured when I perceive a light or an object to be red or white or green I have the impression that this color is a property of the light or the object but what is not immediately clear is whether this red or white or green color is a property of the light or object alternative has been called by some philosophers the common-sense view of color scientific consensus is firmly with the second alternative elements of this scientific view of color can be traced back other possible combinations of two three or more wavelengths a color perception of whitish orange thus tells us nothing about the specific wavelengths present in a light only the overall balance of its spectral Power Distribution color vision is not wavelength detection and specific wavelength composition is not a perceivable property of light Newton showed that what we perceive as the color of an isolated light is the overall balance of its spectral power distribution considered as a two dimensional system we now understand how our vision detects variations in this balance and can see that this two dimensional character is not a physical property of light but is a product of the workings of our visual system this system is called tri-chromatic because it involves three receptor or cone cell types these respond to all all but the longest and the short wavelengths of light respectively and their outputs are compared with each other by a process called Kona ponen C this system allows us to distinguish a two-dimensional circuit of directions of imbalance towards long middle short and long and short wavelengths in contrast a dichromatic species with just two receptor types can only distinguish two directions of imbalance towards long or short wavelengths opponent processing also explains why an even balance of the long middle and short wavelengths is perceived as an absence of hue rather than as three simultaneous hue sensations as we might expect from the way our hearing works our visual system does not detect hue and saturation in light these do not exist as such in light our visual system detects variations in the balance of the long middle and short wavelength components of light and we perceive these variations as variations in hue and saturation when different spectral distributions match in color for example the various whitish orange lights we considered previously they do so because they have the same balance of long middle and short wavelength components from the point of view of the human visual system these graphs show the spectral power distribution z' of five matching white lights the smooth distribution of a CIE daylight illuminant spectral composition of a light or the intrinsic spectral reflectance of an object this leads to the sixty-four million word question does the word color properly refer to the perception in this case white or to the property that evokes that perception in this case an even balance of the long middle and short wavelength components many scientists and some philosophers equate the concept of color with the perception leading them to say simply that colors do not exist meaning that they do not exist physically in lights and objects but only in the mind of the perceiver some other philosophers equate color with the property of the spectral distribution of a light or reflectance of an object that disposes it to appear red or white etc or even with individual physical spectral distributions or reflectances the CIE International lighting vocabulary acknowledges two senses of a word color and provides formal definitions of both perceived color is defined as the characteristic of visual perception that can be described by attributes of hue brightness or lightness and colorfulness or saturation or chroma note that in CIE terminology hue brightness lightness colourfulness saturation and chroma are all defined as attributes of visual perception not physical properties of lights or objects white and various reds and greens are examples of perceive colors a psychophysical color is defined as a specification of a color stimulus in terms of operationally defined values such as 3 try stimulus values and try stimulus values are in turn defined as the amounts of the 3 reference color stimuli in a given tri-chromatic system required to match the color of the stimulus considered a psychophysical color is the measurable property that we use in colorimetry to specify the color of a light or an object it's called psychophysical because it's not a purely physical property but is partly shaped by the visual system of the observer to understand the concepts of psychophysical color and try stimulus values consider your computer screen which is after all the device for generating a light with various long wavelength or our middle wavelength or g and short wavelength or b components imagine viewing your computer screen alongside various areas of the room you are in and matching the light coming from each area by adjusting the amount of the RG and B lights on your screen if your screen is dark enough and you could turn up the lights high enough you could in this way specify the color of the light from most areas you see using a set of try stimulus values consisting of the amounts of the RG and B lights on your screen that matched it you could then plot these amounts as a three-dimensional cubic color space just as your digital camera does for you in its own way automatically you could also plot the ratios of these three try stimulus values as a two dimensional diagram a triangle this two dimensional diagram would show the balance or ratios of the components independent of the total amount of light most lights you see could be plotted in these ways apart from some highly saturated lights including the lights of the spectrum that would lie outside the cube and outside the triangle a chromaticity diagram is a two-dimensional graph that like our RGB triangle shows the ratios of three tri stimulus values independent of the total amount of light the CIE 1931 XY chromaticity diagram is not the latest but is still the most familiar descendant of Newton's color circle you might possibly have encountered one of these diagrams in a technical review comparing the range of colors obtainable on different RGB screens or contained in different RGB color spaces as different triangles the XY chromaticity diagram and a three-dimensional color space CIE XYZ use three purely theoretical primaries x y&z that lie outside the range of actual lights so that their proportions can specify all colors of lights in the XY diagram the colors of the lights of the spectrum follow a horseshoe-shaped line and mixtures of the extreme ends of the spectrum follow a straight line calls a line of purples all actual lights can be matched to points that are enclosed by these lines and can therefore be specified in terms of the proportions of the x y&z components the three trust emulous values do not correspond directly to the amounts of the three wavelength components in a light but it's nevertheless true that lights with high x-values are high in long wavelengths lights with a high y-value a high in middle wavelengths and lights with high Z values are high in short wavelengths and that an isolated light for which x y&z are about equal will appear white to most observers as color vision varies between different observers calculation of understand the difference between perceived color and psychophysical color if we consider contrast phenomena contrast phenomena demonstrate that our color perceptions do not depend entirely on the spectral distribution of a stimulus but are influenced by surrounding areas contrast phenomena are disconcerting to the so called common sense view of color that colors are properties of lights and objects that our visual system simply detects in this example my visual system creates two different perceived colors from squares that match physically and have the same psychophysical color specification our 180g 108 be 108 one of the sixteen point seven million color specifications possible on my screen science necessarily distinguishes between color as a perception and the measurable property of a stimulus that we use to specify its color because the perceived color of a given stimulus can vary not only with the surrounding areas but also with such factors as lighting how long the observer views the stimulus and what they are viewed previously and the individual characteristics of the visual system even the attitude of the observer if you concentrate your attention alternately first on the vertical and then on the horizontal elements of this pattern you may notice that the perceived colors that your visual system creates alternate between vertical reddish and greenish stripes and horizontal blueish and olive stripes now stare steadily at the black dot in the center of the pattern if you're able to keep your eyes very still for long enough you may notice the perceived colors starting to fade to gray at least momentarily your perception of the amount of imbalance diminishes due to adaptation in any case if you've been focusing steadily on the black dot you're probably now perceiving faint colored afterimages and you may again discover that you can change the afterimage colors you perceive by concentrating alternately on the vertical and the horizontal elements but we see colored after images we have a perception of an imbalance that does not exist physically in the spectral distribution of the stimulus again due to adaptation as you continue to look and adapt to the physically uniform area these after image your colors gradually fade and your visual system will eventually create a uniform white perceived color but take this screen into a candlelit room and the perceived color might be distinctly bluish take it outside into sunlight and the perceived color might be a dark gray to sum up when we perceive a light or an object to be red or white or green in color we have the impression that this color is an intrinsic property residing in the light or object and for many purposes it's perfectly reasonable to speak of the light or object as being red or white or green colored as Newton found this is a lot more convenient than constantly talking of red making and green making wavelengths and so on we should bear in mind however that this is speaking not philosophically properly but grossly and is potentially misleading for two reasons firstly scientific consensus is that the red or white or green color does not reside in the light or object but is the way in which we perceive the spectral composition of the light or the spectral reflectance of the object red and green are the ways in which we perceive an overall imbalance towards different parts of the spectrum why does the color of light is the way in which we perceive an overall even balance of spectral composition white as an object color is normally the way in which we perceive a spectral reflectance that is both evenly balanced and very high note though that the perceivable property of a spectral distribution is not its precise physical wavelength composition but is a psycho physical property shaped in part by the visual system of the observer for a tri-chromatic observer this psycho physical property perceived as color relates to the overall amounts of the components of the spectral distribution the familiar two-dimensional circle of hues is a product of the trichromatic visual system and does not exist physically in light secondly the perceived color of a given light or object is not as fixed as it may seem and is influenced by factors relating to the viewing environment and the individual when we measure the color of a light or object using colorimetry we are using the word color in a specific sense based on color matching psychophysical color that captures the disposition of the light or object to appear a certain color to a mathematically defined standard human observer a psychophysical color specification of say an RGB screen stimulus tells us what the stimulus will match it does not equate to a unique true perceived color and can only be converted to a set of perceived color attributes the viewing conditions are specified for example it can be converted to a set of perceived color attributes in the Munsell or NCS system using tables that assume specified viewing conditions or by using a color appearance model to predict its appearance under various other viewing conditions this
